speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Catherine Mahon took place at the Luna House Museum in Lewiston on October 21, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: She reported on a speech that somebody said, one of the speakers said, sorry, but we were not going to be representing Olympia because our representative was a very, very thick man. It would be unable. This was very regretful. Of course, he was that thick, he should resume. Somebody asked me a point. The man had no intention of resigning. He was going to Olympia. While my mother certainly agreed with the man that was doing the talking, it didn't do much good to have him there, but he was going. She repeated, she gave this statement once. The other man jumped him, I am going. You had no right to say that. I'll sue you. He said, I was misquoting. With a very great big law, the A law, then he was misquoting, grossing his quoting. The paper published it to my mother's utter disgust because she felt that she should have been backed up a little bit. But it calmed down a little bit when one man was ministering. He gave an invocation with the thing. He said, well, there was over between two and three hundred people at the banquet. He said it not once, but at least half a dozen times in my memory. He wants 250 people to think he's that much of a liar. I don't think you need to worry. Let him go ahead and sputter. She kind of fumped down about it and thought, well, all right. Let him go.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: What do you have to say?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: She reported taking the head stand. It was too bad we wouldn't be represented. Since representing it was a very sick man, a very sick man. And he had said it was a bit that he wouldn't be. And probably the people they did and then it wasn't true. He had verified his facts and the other man was going to sue him for saying something he shouldn't have said. So he retracted by saying he was misquoting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she work with the interviewers for the very long?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, I was putting the cards to the office. Oh, let's see. Old work of yours probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she enjoy that kind of work?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes, very much. Yes, she ran a paper, a little paper to get her for a while. And she worked in a constant paper when she first came down here. They asked her to work and she did. And then that man went over to the Tribune and she went through it. And that's a while. Yes, she liked it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she actually wrote the Juliaetta paper?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Yes, very much. No, but... And she wrote her first edit for a while. And she was quite pleased with it. She hoped that when the green took on a more somber hue that she would do certain things. And the girl saying the title, that was much... She didn't like this somber hue business, so she said, it takes on a more rosy shade than mother was born by. It wasn't that much she wanted. She was almost into my first editorial in Turland. Because the girl saying the title didn't like the idea of somber hue.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And then, of course, when I grew up around a newspaper someone, there was the arguments that they used to have over the people. And two people were getting married. They were only sitting by their side, wanting the biggest feel. And they would come and tell you something more to make another inch and then bang it in something else. In those cases, you could make the space equal. Because there you were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the substance of the Juliaetta paper, the social news?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, it was large, the local news. You know, local things, because it was a weekly and people would get... There were some comments on national things. I'm sure when we were at war, World War I was on, you'd probably certain things. And there was a general nature too, but you didn't try to compete on the basis of the day base. You didn't belong to a wire service and you were that small a paper. Oh, you didn't get the like the filler from... Oh, you get filler, but you didn't get the wire service. When your mother was in Lewiston, in the trivia in the study, and they began to get their wire service. First, it was just a small little bit. And from that little bit, Mr. Alford, Albert Alford Sr. could make a whole front page. He was very good at it, because he was very convinced, you know. The whole thing was just this kind of telegraph, just extremely condensed. And if one word was wrong, you might lose your meaning. And it just came by a photograph to follow along and responded to Lewiston. It was Lewiston happened to telegraph business, but there was no telegraph here. And so once in a while, if she took it, well, she checked back, but it didn't make sense to say, you haven't got that. You can check back. No, so and so. No, and she would argue back and make them go back and check, maybe back further, back up a little bit on it. But for a while, they had hired somebody that was known, Christian Alford type of thing to take it, but he just took it down. They liked it better when she did it, because she was very careful about it. Interviewing. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Careful about what?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: The wording. So because one word could make a difference in a meaning, you know. When it was such a short brief bit that they were paying for, you paid for how much you got, and they were paying for a small amount. It should be very condensed, and in this condensed state, if you made a mistake, well, it was... And then, and then, and then... From that, they... Alford would take that and rewrite it. Oh, yes.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He was very good. Alford Alford, Sr. was quite a scholar. All his books had been in the finally stolen from the library of Alford Alford. And he really was. He was a scholarly man, very bright, and really quite a brain. And, oh, yes. He was very good at writing. Of course, he did the first one, the second one, the second one, and then he had a lot of stories of the 90s. He liked to talk about the Lewistonians. Spokane didn't, couldn't say Spokane-tonians very well, so they just spoke the nights, and that wasn't as good as Spokane Nights. It wasn't as pretty and as sound, so he liked to play that out. But he was really a scholar, and Red Grilleon was a well -edged baby man, and a Southern John Luke, really. And he was educated in the song. I think it was William & Mary. Oh, yeah. I think that was a sign to call that a school. And I think in the family, that is quite often them a school. I think the father of the present ones, at least, went there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have this paper? Didn't you hear that for a while?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, they did. They used to go up and edit a paper, and one at Kendrick. There was enough money to be made in Lewiston, so they did do some of things, too. One day they were here, and one day they were there. But they were headquartered in Lewiston. They headquartered in Lewiston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when Lewiston was picking up, why didn't they work in Lewiston? Did you hear or understand how they happened to be, do they start with Lewiston?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Not exactly. I mean, there were quite a few people coming west, coming northwest, and Lewiston was kind of a center. They maybe looked at the map, too, and decided, and they came up the head of navigation, as it were, probably came, I think they came in on the boat, with a pen press, probably, and tried it, liked it, and studied. Was it, I wonder if they were, was it to be in the start with, or if they had another... I think it was, but I'm not sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they always have liberal convictions?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, they were, of course, southern, Democrats. However, they were, had been north enough not to have, really, you know, reflecting a great racial prejudice, or anything that way. They were, what's their... Well, here, from Texas, but I think that they had come from farther east than Rachel. And their father was a professional lobbyist. Out for the South. Their father was a professional lobbyist, from Washington, D .C. That is his business. And, um, my sister that I met, I know, lived in Texas, Mrs. Evans, but I think they were from the farther east. I always had a feeling that they were the type that you would find in Virginia. But they had come west. And so, at that time, they came here from Texas. But they were a southern family. And my mother was Mrs. Laura, Mrs. Henry, Henry's Miss Georgia. And they never, you know, always that way. But they were more liberal than some southerners, you see, because they had come north and mixed. And without some of the prejudices that some of the South had. That was quite often true. Now there was a lawyer here, Mr. Reed. And James Reed was from the South, too. And he was from, I think, South Carolina. And they were very strong friends. And they were pretty much alike. Brains educated southern people. And now he was much more liberal. But when his daughter was up here, she was from the old, she was the old South. And a black, as we would say now, a negro, really, she thought they should step off and stand in the gutter till she walked by. That was, they were, her feeling very definitely. They were not, you know, anything that way. But now her father didn't have that feeling. He had lived in the north. He had become more liberal. And of course they stayed Democrats. And the Democratic Party is more or less a liberal party. But they had become more liberal on race and quite a few other things. Well, they were active in the early days in Lewis and Affairs. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Well, they didn't run for office, but they were keenly interested. Oh, yes. Always keenly interested in the community. I think they, as I said, they didn't themselves run for office, but they were very keenly interested. Oh, yes. And they liked certain people. And the Democratic Party was very strong. No, no. It certainly was one thing on the north. Well, no, no, it wasn't. It wasn't so strong. They were in my north. And it may have been something about supporting the underdog, too. One man that my mother knew very well came through here. He was editor of the poultry journals and things. He came judging at Thayer's, you know. He was also Southern gentleman. And he said he always belonged to minority parties, but he was in Louisiana. He had been a Republican. But as soon as he owned, over the Pierce County, he was a Democrat because the Democrats were in the majority. And it may have been a little of that, but I think the thing was, too, they, you know, that was inappropriate. That was the only party.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother have any opportunity at the college?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yeah. Um, well, probably. There weren't colleges around the same. When she was in politics, she went through what was called Spokane College. But it was just like going to high school. And the Boughlin Linsing graduate didn't do anything with you. Consider that we were the ones that went out to preach. It was a Methodist, I think, college. And after she had come to Lewiston, I know she thought she could quit and go to normal. But the job wouldn't be as good as what you had when she got through, so she stayed with what she had. Um, Moscow was just really a new college. Pullman was a new college. She remembered when it was located. Uh, you see, they, when they were going to locate Pullman, she was living in Pullman's. And for this, though, they would get the college. He was going to go to Whitman Caldwell County. And, um, actually, the way of the brown was the best of Pullman's, you know. But they thought, well, we'll get it. So they wanted a good price for their land and so forth. Colfax got wanted, but they didn't have a good location for it in their old valley. And they had the county seats. And Pullman really got in and worked. And they offered them this, that, and the others that were located. So they went there. And, of course, at that time, Moscow started then. And it was a small, it was a rather small school, too. They went on to play things called colleges that weren't real. They weren't real at colleges. Lewis didn't have one in the early times. It was completely gone when she came here. And he was going to call the college and, um, present Turner. And his son had taught school at Palouse and boarded it at my grandmother's place. And she had attended his school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But— Oh, has he called the college?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes. His name had been Turner. And one of his daughters married a Brooklyn, Memphis salesman. They were rather cultured people. But I questioned how much of a college it was. It was a— It was private, then. Yes, it was private. And there were a lot of those around, but actually, I don't think that they were too much, you know. They were lit, hammered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did she meet your father?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, she was sent by the telephone company to Burns, Oregon. The company was buying up land. I mean, buying up land, but little companies. And they bought that one. And the man that was the Northwest Tractor manager, Mr. Bush, liked my mother very much. He was considered quite difficult. They were all afraid of him, more or less. But my mother was rather precise. I guess he didn't have much to hide. He liked him. And one of the ladies, they want a man from the And it was. It was a good job. This was a relative that he'd done there. Well, they were sending a relative here. To be the man. He had too big an idea as to about being a manager. He had to make up the money he took. So after while her chief operator was the manager, they went back to work. One was in a kind of long, normal position. Oh, well, yes. Well, by a man. They said, you know, there weren't too many jobs open for women. And they thought that a man for a little problem, as I said, they burned their fingers on that one. Quite a bit. Because of course, he being a relative of two of the officers in the company, they didn't like him. They can't attend to it. They made up the money when he didn't stay as manager. But in the upset, my mother had gone to take this job. And her job would be to go in when they bought the company and standardized it and put it into their shape.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this where she got it, where she had the hard time getting the roof?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes. It burned. But the next place... She went and took that company out. The next place she would have gone was in the Yakima. You see, that was... It was being... The company was renewed then. She must have been a pretty good man in your area. Oh, she did have. Yes, she did have. She could train an operator and she could standardize the books and do all that. Oh, yes. So she usually goes into the visual for women at the time to have... Well, to get into the position to do the... Well, there weren't too many opportunities. Let's say it that way. There were plenty of women probably that could do things, but there weren't too many opportunities. There weren't so many. There were not very many places open. There were women doctors, sure. You could be a doctor or you could be a lawyer, but it was very unusual. Had a pretty hard time in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the qualities of your mother that made her a business woman at that time?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, probably. This may sound funny, but she had a great adult who could be very exact in detail. And she was a great... She was a very energetic worker. I mean, she was very persistent.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: She said, of course, she stayed there. She was very determined to go on and finish anything she did. Very, very determined, not easily. Turned aside. And you know her mother was a business woman. She'd become a milliner, which was one of the few things open.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Maybe it was a little bit of the... You know, the Quaker women were in it that way. They were inclined to do that. And they were from a Quaker heritage to a degree. And you look back at the Quakers and they were... Every once in a while the women did quite a bit. They were rather persistent and they did do quite a few things. They were strong suffragists, you know. It was a Quaker trait. And while my mother wasn't a Quaker and her mother, she wasn't raised with it. Still, there was that feeling. And they was in the background, I guess. A woman had the same rights as a man.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And they were realists. They would do what they could. They wouldn't try to do something that they couldn't do, you know. If I can't do this but I can't do that, all right, you'll do that. That's good, too. So you think your mother was quite aware of limitations and... She had the labor of her being. Oh, yes, of course. And it was dominated. Oh, she knew that. Of course, they all knew that. But still, you know, she always voted because women here were given rights. We never had the struggles that they did in Texas or New York or some of those places. Women out here in the West were given more rights. My mother always voted. And all that was nothing she had a struggle for.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, they were so much better than they were in other places. And, of course, women have always been able to... All right, I can't do that very well. I won't get along very well with the council, so I'll get a man to go and do what I wanted to do. Women have done that, and they still do. You know, I mean, because if they'll listen very recently, as long as he puts out the ideas I want, fine. And we do it to this day. You know, women do. If it's going to be better, and if you're paying for an ad on TV, you may not want to do the talking yourself. If you think a man's voice is going to be more pleasing. Okay, let him go ahead and sell it, but it's my article, or it's what I want. So...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For your mother to get married, was that a difficult decision for her to make between me and giving up a career?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, no. No, she had quit. And her father wasn't very worth... You know, was getting old, and she had enough means that she thought she could man me. She had quit anyway, working. She was working when she met my father. But she wasn't working away from home at that time. No, and she had been engaged before that. She was engaged before that. He died at one of the Buffalo Hutt Boys. The Buffalo Hutt Boys, you know. And... Oh, no, that was something that she expected, probably, to be married. My impression has been that for a lot of women, especially for instance the teachers, well, there isn't any contract, I don't know what it was, possibly in the top district, even there, you can't teach anymore. I mean, it seems to me that in that act, kind of a lot of women were forced to choose. Oh, yes. That was more or less a little bit the way it was when I was teaching, a little bit, you know. Sure. As some of them, they were married when I was teaching. But, I mean, it was a little easier. You might get a job teaching in your home district if they knew you, and they think, well, now Wilmette and Sosans will do this. They can now keep the money at home and she's a good teacher. But, I mean, if you were applying for a job away from there, it probably entered into it.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And, because they would say, well, is he going to live here or walk or, how are they going to do it? Is she going to be going home every weekend or watching?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, he was a little dead. He was always considering it. He was probably a handicap in certain jobs. And then during the Depression, there was a feeling about one member of a family having a job. And they should be the man at Boston. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. And so, Wilmette was spreading it around. And, of course, women, under those times, sometimes had difficulties, especially if she were married and still wanted a career in work. I think she was in trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But your mother, the idea of being restricted to the sphere at home, didn't explain that?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, no, no, no, no, no, no, no. She knew just as she pleased after she was married just as much as she would have. She was a bit worried about that, I don't think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father was in a band, too?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes, he was. Never thought that way. They worked together right here. But, no, he might not like it, or she might not like it. But that was... Oh, no, it never occurred to him to be that way. Well, it seems like a lot of men had expectations in their lives. Oh, yes. They missed and they're not going to do that. Well, he didn't. No. He had a very definite feeling that women's rights should be affected. And remember, he had just a daughter, and a daughter that he thought a lot of. And he wanted that daughter to be treated just the way he wanted to. So he would defend women's rights. You see, that's probably why we don't have more conflict. Because after all, you know, all the women have a father. They probably saw a heck of a lot of them. And maybe the women have a son that they're very concerned with. And they want that son that privileges. So you see, it balances out. I mean, there's that about it.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Wasn't it, Mariah? Theresa of Austria, whose father wanted her to be able to have the throne, you know?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And he did everything he could because it was against the law, but he got it changed. Because he had a daughter and he wanted her to be able to succeed him. See, they all... It all comes in. Well, it should. It should. But it comes in more from father and daughter and mother and son, probably. That it does from a husband and wife in lots of cases because it probably could be more conflict there. So you do... I'll take care of you now. You do what I say.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But how did this want somebody bossing his daughter that way, you know?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He wants that... His daughter is very special. That's true of you, your father. Oh, my goodness. Oh, my goodness. He adored him.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And of course, and I was an only child. So no way would he want anything to be against the rights of women. That would be affecting his most precious thing, you know. So of course not. But as I say, I don't think the women in this area had as much problems as they did in some of those cases. I think that Mrs. Babb, the lawyer's wife, I know she said, well, you know, Catherine, we had it on a silver platter, always. We always had privileges and rights. And it was true. And so then you were thinking of it as a Western... Well, it was very technical of the West, and it wasn't technical of all of it. Now, there were probably spots, but no, I think that was more Western. A rate of freedom. Actually, anybody that came with us, the women shared all the hardships, and were expecting then the results of it. And they homesteaded too, remember. And they took up land. Maybe husbands and wives together, so they have a bigger range, but then they had their rights. And women -grant businesses in the West, they probably did in the East, but there weren't so many other businesses in the town. Now, the one thing they didn't do in the beginning was, in the stores, they can clerk. They were men. You bought your ribbons from a man. It was a bomber hired women in the store, but a lot of the stores, they were just a gang to a little bit in the 90s and the West stores. Well, probably it was an economic thing because it was a general store, and maybe they had to sell the ribbon and move the barrel of sugar. You see, it probably had an economic thing, but we think of clerks so often, so many of the clerks as being women, but they were in the beginning there. They were very few women clerks, except in maybe in the middle of the store. But the women ran hotels, and in Lewiston, Annie Trimble ran the saloon. They was hanging Annie's saloon, but Annie ran the saloon because Hank had to stay on the ranch and decide he might think. So she ran the saloon because she was. You know, I mean, it happened. The women did run a lot of businesses, but the one thing they didn't do that in my young time, that one job open to women was to be a clerk. That was one of the things you could do. It sounds like the reason, and do you think the reason was that way, because being in the West and just opening up, there was all this possibility for people to fill jobs. I think that had a lot to do with it. I think it was out of the gate. And there's always been a greater freedom out here. I can remember when I met with the dead East, and this didn't serve to use New York, and I was coming West to my uncle. I was 16, and he was telling me how to behave on the way, and now if a strange man approaches you, you freeze them with a look. You are not to speak to strangers. Strangers don't speak to you, they are not to be worried. And wait, that was true to Chicago, or I was arranged for me to be met with friends. So I had a nice time in Chicago. Then to Omaha, and that was another overnight trip. And then coming West, everybody on the car knew everybody, and I would freeze them with a look in my eye. There was a father and a man that knew about all the different interesting things, and he talked to people. There was one mother and a woman that was kind of that way. There was one young girl my age, and we went for lunch together. We had a grand time, freezing with a look. Everybody knew everybody, and everybody talked. All of a sudden, I was fine, I was home, I was West. But nobody talked to you that day. You didn't need to worry about freezing me by the look. There wasn't anything doing this. Strangers were strangers. You didn't speak.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: We just went home. Yes.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, well, now isn't that something to look at? Look at this, and how about that?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Where are you from, and I'm so -and -so. It was just a different theater. Oh, yes, very, very different. And of course, I can remember, I was going to Seattle, a friend of mine who never thinks I'm paying enough attention to my parents.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I asked her, and she said, Now how can you be sure of your hair and your gloves?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Be sure of your hair. After all, you know Seattle is much more of a city, and you won't be. And I was there, and I had an old time, and I was looking at Freddie T. Nelson's window after hours. And I remember the woman next to me, and I remember looking at her, and it was wartime, more than wartime.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And she looked at something in common about sucking the window very naturally, and she said, I'm from Dakota.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Where are you from?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Because people brought me up to talk. You don't have the strangers in Seattle, and you wear your hats, you wear your gloves. Oh, boy. And we were West, and it was ridiculous, you know. There is a Western feeling. I don't think that you do everything, but I think Westerners are different, and I know that Mrs. Taylor, Mrs. Taylor was, oh, excuse me, Mrs. Taylor, Mrs. Taylor is a patron of, and she talked about, talking about going on a trip, and they had gone, I think, to Alaska, and how different you could tell the Western people right away from the history, because we talked. We were more friendly. Yeah, I suppose it's howdy, stranger, but it's our way of knowing it. Well, it's part of the West that any strange person came through your door. They probably had come a long ways on the ranch, and it was the correct thing to invite them in to share hospitality. You always offered them a night's lodging and food, and if you didn't, there was something very wrong with you, and you were very unpopular, you know. And that was the old West, and I suppose it's an outgrowth event. But we have had that. You had to be rather open and friendly if you were going to survive. I wonder if that had something to do with the easier time, the way you were doing that. I think so. I think it was the whole thing, the whole bit.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: It doesn't have to be much more open out here. You were talking about the relationship with your father as being a close one. Oh, that is. I'm interested in it. I've been on the child too, and I have a good coddle by my family. Well, you were on the child to give me responsibility. I remember that. As only child has the responsibility when time has come, if there's a family and mother's sick, there's two people or three or four to make the decision. If you're an only child, maybe you are front and center alone, and there is a lot more responsibility with being an only child. It isn't all about it. I remember my teacher, Keene, he said, all the only children hold up their hands. Now look at all the spoiled children. I didn't know what to say then. I didn't know how to handle them. So I said nothing. But I would say now look at all the responsible ones. They're going to have to face a lot of decisions. You, the only children, are in a family very responsible because you are a get -to. You have to front and center. An only child has the face right from the beginning that they only hope that folks have in there. If they disappoint them, just think what it is. If there's two or three, well, let Susie do it, or let John do it. But when you're the only one, it is front and center.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that makes for a closer relationship with the parents too, as a small family unit?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I don't know. I think that's individual differences probably. But the difference, we're talking about the special closeness between the father and daughter. Very interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You feel that that is a pattern in a lot of families, or do you think it was just unusual?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, I think there are other fathers, but I think mine was especially good that way. But I think there probably are other fathers, I know of some, that just simply... Adore them. Oh, take Mr. Earl. His life is wrapped up in their daughters of complete history.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: It's just such a natural thing, you know?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: My Valerie is everything to him. Just everything. She has to her mother too, but very, very special for a family. I know I said to Tony, I always knew if I did something just terrible, I would go to my folks and they would help. But I always knew that my father would say, I don't give a bad job, they will help you.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And my mother would say, what in the world did you do that for?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: We'll help you. But she would say, and she said, well, that's the other way it would be with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager

So that's why I really want to keep some of them a little bit equal. Well, in the affection in the family, now a lot of families, I've had this impression, there were a lot of families that really believed that you shouldn't show affection to your kids. That it was important to raise them with a sense of discipline. That's a rather whole country, I think. I think that probably varies in the country, because it wasn't an Irish way. My father was Irish, and he would remember how much affection there was in his family. That his father could come in, he ran a quarry. And he had stayed on too long and got his feet cold, and how his father would rub them with snow, which probably wasn't just for him, we know now, but he thought it was. I never complained and do all these things for them. I never complained a bit, or anything that way. So affection was a thing, it was in the family. All my clan, father and mother's father thought they loved his family. It was very affectionate toward them, and his wife, who hadn't had it because she had not been raised in the kindness, but she wanted her children to have it. I don't know which he was a very nice girl. And, well, come on there and hurry up. And he was just real cross with her. And never any. And my father would have said how nice I was to bring him his lunch. I would have been an angel, absolutely, and if he had a nickel and a habit, for Ice Cream Corner some reward, then oh, he would have made a bit to do over me. He wasn't that hard on the child. I don't fire him. You know, it was about his feeling about him. He didn't like that at all. And I never liked it, of course. Never liked it when a man imposed it. I remember that there was a couple in Burns. The man hung around the saloons, and he said, he'd see his wife out in the snow splitting kindling wood or splitting wood. And my father thought that was the worst of all, if a man didn't split the wood, because they had to help the wood to burn. And the woman had to, and that was just simply the most. That was absolutely disgraceful. And for the ranch, they had to hire a cook and rust about. And she was the only one they could get. And he came into town, and he hired her and her husband. He said, well, at least for once he's got to split the wood. While he's working out on the ranch, he has to keep that. So she doesn't split the wood, at least for once. Work was planted in life. Libby Street with Clarkston, his name for him and so forth.

speaker: Catherine Mahon

And they met in the telephone office because everybody had business in the telephone office. And my mother was still engaged because Mr. Butterfield hadn't died yet. He was very poorly. But my father liked her very much, right from the beginning, I guess. So he did everything he could. Of course, it was family joke that the first thing she asked him for was beer. She couldn't drink the alkali water of Burns. It was just so alkali. And she thought maybe she could drink some beer. And she said in Lewiston, if she'd want some beer, she could have sent down to one saloon to a messenger boy and got it. But I'm in trouble, but she didn't know Burns that way. And she was so thirsty. And she thought maybe she could drink some beer. And she thought he was in there, a young man, and wouldn't take time to do advantage of her request for beer. Of course, he always kidded her about it. And then I know he liked this new telephone girl. He had been used several times to his brothers in Syracuse. So he probably had a little more city ideas than some of the boys. And she was best probably a little more city because she came from Portland there.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And I know that one time one of the traveling men coming through had caught some special fish. He wanted to ask my father to take it in and get it cooked. He said, now, you can have one of them. This is for me. Or something. Anyway, it was to be for him. So my father took the fish and gave it to the girl at the hotel. And he said, Florence, you can have that one, but this is for Miss Burns. So my mother said, no, the difference. I'm probably right in front of this family man. You had the very ordinary fire of probably beef steaks. There's an old sea tender and a kind of kind of range, basically. And here she was eating this nice trout. She didn't know. And he thought that was a hilarious deal. But, yeah. And she didn't know that she was getting the note. Oh, well, she knew yet, but she didn't know what this was. But she thought it was just the biggest joke ever was to have this man come in. Not that one's research. It's somebody who knew real well. And, but he never, when they called, called. And the man, he then, they corresponded. It was four years later before they were married. So, but that's where they met. Of course, I asked him, I said, now my dad, I know my mother walked across one of those muddy streets and fell about her skirts. She had a very nice ankle, extremely nice ankle. It was as good as Marlena Diggrick or Betty Graber or Eddie Grins. And I said, I know. Because he really always knows this pinacle's on people. And women didn't show their ankles much then. And, all right, that was a joke from my family. But he thought she was just a top right. Four years and largely my correspondence. Oh, not my correspondence. They come to see her once in a while, but you know, he's in Alaska. Most of the time. They had met there in Oregon and he came out from Alaska and had come to see her. But it was quite a bit of correspondence.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He was rather an idealist. He could idealize anybody. And she was, he thought she was just a top right. That way. And they were running different in temperament, very different. Different from calm. Oh, and and he's in lots of ways, but there is always this great respect. You know, they wouldn't shock each other too much. Oh, she might cut her hair. One of the first to bop her hair.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And see what he said. He actually had a haircut two years before I knew. And, well, I hope you're satisfied. He doesn't look like him. Well, maybe you could do about it. He does that. But still, basically, they would always respect each other's feelings. And important things count on the other one, you know.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Neither one would boss the other too much. Because that wasn't what you did. I would never crazy my father to tell my mother, you can't do that. Because I say you can't. I'm at the house. They would never talk anything that way over. I suppose there were, there are homes where that was done. It always comes as a surprise to me. Because I think anything matters should be a mutual discussion. But I know there are homes where it isn't. And in those days it must have been even more common. Well, there were people that talked things over though and did it very differently.

words: Oh, very much. I think anyone, and I think Irish men can too, probably. You know, I think naked they do. But, of course, my grandfather who is Dutch, Luxembourg Dutch, was certainly idealized as mine. And when it was perfect. Elmo, he called her Elmi. She was the perfect Elmo. It seems like a pretty good basis for a marriage. Well, friendship is probably better than idealizing. It's hard to live up to be an ideal. And I think friendship is probably better, but then it was rather a thing. And no one ever took Elmo's clothes. She died by seeing Elmo would look at another woman.

words: With your parents, do you think it was friendship eventually?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, quite a bit. Oh, yes, quite a bit. And mutually, it just knew me. Very much that. I think that children do have a tendency to hold people. But in a lot of families, I think it was the roles, you know, the role of the wife. I suppose. But no, it wasn't so much that. I don't think so. I don't think so. Of course, my Irish grandmother, I didn't know Catherine Foley Nail. I dare say it was... Oh, she was from the leadership in the South Valley. You were married. You were married for life, definitely. That good Catholic, oh my goodness.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But I think more...

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: That's the building character, probably. You know, not being a fivy person.

words: I'm not good in words. It was a great spirit to play in my mother and also in my father, but we'd come out differently.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, a different business would probably have done lots of things.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But no, they had a lot in common too. A great deal in common. He was more conservative. Yeah, he certainly didn't like it. I was thinking about him saying that he disliked Roosevelt. Oh, no, he didn't like Roosevelt. He didn't like Roosevelt. He didn't like Jackson. He didn't like anything but he'd say war. He'd say war. War. He didn't like anything about it. But you know, when you think about the Democrats having had such a bunch of losers for so long except for Wilson. He didn't like Wilson. They finally came up for the winner. He didn't like Wilson either. He was surely a good Democrat until they were in the office and he didn't like him. He didn't like Wilson.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: it was a good way to start the day up here in some of the early days too because it wasn't all that much money in an orchard. They had an orchard. And it was a very beautiful life. There's nothing more tempting than an orchard and growing things. And it's not a profitable smile. Very few people make anything on it. Can do is a little better, but fruit, soft fruit is a touchy business.

words: Very uncertain. It always is. It still is.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: There's a difference. Quite a difference. A very great difference.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, you have to sell your fruit. You take an earner.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: What did you give me for it?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: It's the what will you give me. You have no sense to bargain. You take what you can. Sometimes it's a bill for transportation. My father always felt sorry for the growers. He only prayed simply for them even when he was on the other side and buying.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And did I tell you the time he had the men from Chicago here at Juliaetta?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, he was friend of the camera and the camera always contacted the large orchard to fruit. The man came out from Chicago. And wanted to rent while he came out. And he had run a wholesale house in Chicago where you shipped fruit, you know, and they sold on a discount basis, you know, and a percentage basis. And he had handled fruit from Juliaetta. He remembered handling it. This man walked in and he had diamonds, diamond rings, and he's a bookkeeper with him. He was dressed very nice, all heart -shaped remarks. And he hung up the camera. He said he thought he would take a vacation unless he was going to do the pay for his vacation. And he said he thought he would pack out some cars of fruit and ship them back to his house in Chicago.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And was it fruit he could get that year in Juliaetta?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And my father had been worrying all season about the potter orchard because the potter orchard was, he didn't have it under contract, but he knew the people so well. He said, I'll have to take it. And it's such a big crop this year and they're going to be so small that I can't do it with profit.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: What will I do with the potter orchard?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: A big gallon can grade. And so this man walked in and he sat out of the blue and he said, I'd like to pack out some fruit.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Is there any extra fruit that you don't have under contract?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And dad said, yes, there's fruit. I have enough under contract to fill our orders. And there is other fruit. He said, well, I've always thought the potter orchard fruit was so good. I've had it there in Chicago.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Is it?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, you can get the potter orchard.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He said, well, where would I pack?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: He said, well, I can rent you space because you'll be through before we start canning. You can use this warehouse space and you can use the same crew. Largely they work for you and then they'll come and work for me. So he wanted to see the potter orchard and they rented a horse and buggy, which he wrote in those days. And without him looked at the trees. And my father held his breath because he thought, oh, my, he's really going to take it. But the man just saw trees loaded with bright red cherries and thought, oh, this is lovely. And he tasted them. And of course, they did have a fresh good taste. And I always thought this was lovely. But mostly he just looked at them and thought it was grand. So he bought the potter orchard. And my father got ahold of the man in the town. He said, get every picker in town in there fast because there's going to be an awful squawk pretty soon. And so everybody in the little town of Juilliard that could pick cherries went down and picked the potter orchard and all the people that could pack showed up. Because you know, it doesn't take much in a word of mouth in the town of 400. And pretty soon the man from Chicago, who had been very pleasant to Mother and I, was going to these currants your husband so big because they were so small they wouldn't face out because they used to pack them very fancy to face the fruit. They had to put them in a little cart. Of course, they didn't lose any money on it, but they had agreed, my father don't get a price per pound. And they had agreed to it and they had contracted the old tree. And they got their money and the fruit was shipped. And of course they could sell them in Chicago all right. Well, he had bought the fruit and he didn't realize it was so small. It was very small because it was such a big crop. When there's a big crop, your fruit is small. And my father said, well, I'm an expert on fruit, but I would go out with a can and I would count the number of cherries in it. Because when you put out a good can of cherries, we can for Sprague Warner and company, the world's largest hotel, grocery store, grocery place.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: We had to have, for this grade, just so many cherries had to fill a can. Say there was 80 and if you had 100, you were subgrade. And it had to fill it. It had to waste so much after it was drained, say for half an hour, the syrup had to test a certain amount. And it had to come up to these very strict standards. Well, you had too small a cherries, you couldn't put in this grade. They went in a poorer grade, down to the gallon. And this was what would have happened to those cherries. They had tremendous crops, a weight, but they were small.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was the Potter orchard in the same shape everybody else was in there?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, it was just the Potter orchard. The rest of them weren't so bad. Oh, the Potter orchard had a bigger crop. Bigger crops than usual. It had just sat heavily and it was a mess. A fruit stand point, it was too many. And of course you don't think cherries, so there it was. So it got better in this man. Oh, much, everybody in Judea did well. It wasn't really the money that didn't come to father. It came to everybody in the town. But of course everybody was chuckling. The country hicks had put it over on the city slicker. There was just a case of that, because there was this man and everybody that was in the business could remember times when they had sold to these houses and they felt not gotten a fair deal. My folks had sent lettuce to Spokane once when they, it was a cat crop in the greenhouse. And they said, oh, we're so glad to get the lettuce. We have no other lettuce in Spokane. The price wasn't up a bit. The mother said, well, we thought we'd get a little more lettuce. The only lettuce, oh, we didn't want to hold up our people. The house, of course, did, but then proven. So, a nick -match on. And it was that all the time. And you would get a, you would chip your fruit and they'd say, well, it was spoiled. It was this, it was that. And sometimes you didn't feel it could possibly be that bad. And there was always a feeling that they were cheated. So everybody was chuckling when they got the best of the man who was flashing around there in diamond rings, to the honest toils, workers of the soil, the other people.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, mother had an orchard when she married father. She already had a house on the place.
