words: And of course there was a great deal of prejudice and some things about them like people would eat the food they raised because Really?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh well, yes. The way they fertilized it. It was waste matter, you know. It was that. But Chinese laundry, my mother had her shirts, shirt waste that she wore to work in. And they did a good job. And she paid them, and it was 25 cents a shirt, which was a lot more than those they heavily, heavily starched. Boy, it must have been an awful town.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: There was, they kept it themselves. I suppose they were a thing. Now some people, there was one or two girls, there was one girl here that could translate. The Chinese in court, and she talked to them more. She was a nice person. But I suppose there was a lot of abuse and criticism. I think they were probably taking the danger of, as our Indians were too. You know, there was always people to exploit them. The Chinese were smart, but they had a hard time. Of course there were Chinese cooks now in every big ranch and every hotel. They had a Chinese cook. And sometimes they were very much a part of that household. There was a Chinese cook at the Raymond House, there was a Chinese cook at the Imperial and spoke in Grangeville. And in a big ranch, and they had a cook, quite often it was a Chinese cook, like a French or French flam that spit in the, he had a Chinese cook. And that was very typical. The cooks were Chinese. But we all know they were terribly exploited in that place. They were, I have heard that the family that I know in law school told me that pretty much what they had to do to make a living in their restaurant was to offer more. Or offer better repair, offer it for less money, or all of them for people to patronize a Chinese restaurant instead of an American one. So what it meant was they had to just work a lot harder to make a living. Yes, I think they did. I think that is true. I think that was very true. And the Japanese were a little more aggressively independent. Of course, the Japanese had been in my line of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there some here in Lewiston?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: In World War. Yes, there were a few. There are a few Japanese families here. And they are very well thought of. But they were resented some. I remember though, there was this one, and it is very well thought family in North Lewiston, and the teacher over there was in the normal, and she said they were so appreciative of their children going to school, you know, that they came to the school board and wanted to pay extra. And they said, no, the school is free, you know. And they just could hardly believe it. And she said they were very nice children, and they have become very Americanized. But there were some people that would make remarks, and the children fought in World War II. They weren't interned here in Lewiston, but there were some that, oh, yeah, you know. And of course, they were very badly treated being interned. And it was so ridiculous. Now, they weren't interned in Hawaii, where they had been attacked by them. Oh, sure, there were some of the few that were known to be loyal to Japan, but otherwise they weren't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I know one nurse told me, she said, well, talking about it, they said, well, if there were more came more, what would you do?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, she had to go to her post, because she was a nurse.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, what would you do with your baby?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, the nurse would take care of it. The nurse was Japanese. And she said, why, she was a defender of that child with her life. I didn't know why. And she said, I couldn't get that idea over. But they didn't feel any different in Hawaii.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: If they didn't, then why should we?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: You know, we.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this kind of an approach to school? Or was this before the Second World War?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was when they were first from?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yes, and they thought maybe they should do something more for the school, because they, so please.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think the living Japanese men here?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They front part.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, do they?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They front part. Because they take to growing things. The Chinese are more apt to run restaurants and long -raising things that way. And the Japanese are greenhouse people or truck farmers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much to do with them in the early years?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Didn't know them. I didn't know them. I met them late. Didn't know them at all. But everybody that did like them, they were in another part of town. We didn't know them at all. I was wondering about when they came to America. I don't know. They were very good friends with the Italian truck car members. Because they both worked hard. And of course I can speak the same language when they had the truck farmers. I know enough about it. I can talk to them, you know. Well, I have a thing in common with growers, you know. And I meet the Japanese in, when we go to one of our conferences, there will always be some. And they're very successful and they're very good. Some of them are very much, even now, someone of the old world, their country custom though. After they've been here longer, not so much. Now there are two brothers that own one of the largest ranges for certain things in Seattle, in the world. It's world famous. And they're Japanese. They are tall. They're bigger than the average. We think of the Japs as small. But these boys are tall. Somebody said, well, they're from northern Japan. And actually they're rather shy. They are inclined to blush when they talk. But very well educated. They went to Washington State, to floriculture.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They brought their wives from Japan. Now they are young. That is, they wouldn't be young to you, but they would me. They're younger than I am. And I was at one of our conventions and there was a very attractive woman there whose husband grew orchids. And I thought she was the most charming person. And from a look standpoint, in the whole room, she was fatigued, dark, and very pedestrian. And we were talking and she said, look, look. And just like something out of a painting for these two, they brought their wives down. They kept them very much apart. They didn't mingle at all. Some of the other Japanese women do in the fifth. They didn't mingle at all. And they ate. We were having a barbecue dinner. It was out in the open, rather far. They came by themselves. And I suppose it was almost, they were made up heavily, almost like a noun. And they just, like, they stepped out of a kitchen. And I think they came straight from Japan and married. I think it was one of those deals. And here these boys were college graduates and all. And I know I talked to a man who lived a lot, and had learned to speak. And then lived at college with some of the Orientals. And he knew them very well. And he said, well, I said, he looked kind of autocratic and embossing. He said, well, after all, you know, would you turn your son loose in a certain thing that he digs, it's mistakes, and it's more protection. They were very proud of him, I think. And very proud. It was, I didn't get to meet them, really, at all. They were very shy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did these boys come from here to China?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: I don't know. They lived there in Seattle. Their grinch is there. And they probably were second generation. Their folks must have come. They're the age that they would be second generation. And very successful. They forced the more points as any other firm in the world. And they are, they'll ask questions, and they will once somehow prepare a paper that a college professor will read at our meetings. And what they knew was just fabulous, but they are, they're Japanese. And their wives, look, just like they and I understand, they came from Japan to marry them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know that there were the blacks around here?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Not really. We were one, of course, everybody knew Pete, but there was another man riding shoeshine kites, and his boy left high school to play football very well. And they laughed when he was through. So that they could be in another place for him to go on to school. And I think he met Precious. And there was none of the clocks that time. And they said that he was one of the best players. But he wasn't getting his letter as soon as the others. And when it came to, there was no piper to dance with. There was nothing, you know. And generally they want them to do before they have others. And I think it was Precious. And I think it was Precious that his father had to be a shoeshine kite.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the guy that was on the main?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They both were, but Blue Pete was the more known one. But this wasn't that. He ran the place farther out. And I think they were very nice. I think his wife had been a teacher. Blue Pete was the other one. Blue Pete held his own. Blue Pete held his own in a way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he your girlfriend?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes. He was your dad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And who was she trying to make, Pete?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Like, why?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: But Blue Pete held his own more. But the other fellow was a little more quiet and had a son who they left for him to be educated. He might have learned and got into college, and he might have gotten into a profession or something. So Blue Pete was well known. Oh, everybody was Blue Pete. They said he held his own. More. More. Well, Blue Pete was interested in money, of course. Supposed to have served a tournament in jail for one white man that took the blame of us. And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it's a bootlegging business?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Yeah. Blue Pete was a gambler. He had a heck of a time, too. He had fun. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does it look like?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Oh, yeah. It's very interesting. But probably... It's probably rough on his wife. I think she was kind of a nice person and probably wouldn't have too many friends. I think they had a tough time here. Not that there was too much intentional thing, but there was nobody that we mixed with, and we didn't mix with that much. And, you know, we had... When the DuPaur Orchestra came through, or choir came through, that was after World War II. Quite a while after. But they had been in it. And they were Negro, were black.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And... One of the teachers said, don't book them, we'll have too much trouble. You know, with the racism, and they said, why, we have commercial prejudice here. It's all right. Go ahead. And they were marvelous. It was in our concert series, you know. And I went, and I know this was being talked in the group that I was with back then. And when the fellows came in, and they came in through the front door, and we were lined up to go and... Excuse me, excuse me, going in, and there was a smile in the group.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I thought, why are there a serious bunch?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: They were just very aloof. Excuse me. Go ahead.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And all they sang beautifully, beautifully, in the one chord. They didn't think that. When they sang a solo, he did. But for the group, no. They were kind of normal, sorry, because they had just come out of the room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it called?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: DuPaur. P -A -U -R, something. DuPaur, I think. DuPaur, duPauw.

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And... Oh, it was just a heavenly night for music. Thank you. We didn't know anything about it. Finally, we were just not going to leave. We were just so excited. Though I asked them, we were clapping for the whole group. And they came back, and they sang. They took care of us beautifully. They sang the stars, thank them, and they had to stand up. They got us out. And we, by that time, got the message that for some reason, we were not the favorite audience. But we enjoyed them much. Found out in the morning paper they hadn't been given decent housing. And I was very glad that the only housing that was, that was first -class that was offered them was the motel next to me in Clackston. Otherwise, it was very second -class. And I knew the people that were responsible for it. They were somewhat friends. And they had to, no, we won't take them. They're blacks. And I can see that maybe no one hotel would take the whole 40, I think it was 40 of them, because it was probably quite a group. But they could only take in some.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this a decision made by the hotel owners in themselves?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: And the motel owners, that they wouldn't do it. And the men that was headed down the phone, and they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was after World War II?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: After World War II. And that was pretty recent. And I know that a man that told me the whole story, and I didn't know it all, came out in the paper, you know, that they weren't. That was, you know, knew why, that they weren't, why they were mad at us. And didn't blame them. But that was, there was this spike in people housing, you know, was coming up. And it was being pushed, and it was a real thing. But it was later than I knew, because this, I don't know why I'm saying the same word, but this organization, you know, if you tried to get the reservations, and so on, it was a whole bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All this business with equal housing, was that, you mean a local issue?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: Well, it was national. I mean, it was coming on that time. Civil rights. Civil rights were coming on, and not even yet. Of course, now that couldn't happen. You don't exclude somebody for a race of color, for you. But at that time, you couldn't. And at that time, there would be the signs white only. Well, in so far as live, your family was much less prejudiced than most, and really didn't have any, or had very good relationships. Very, they weren't conscious of me. They didn't intend to have any. I think unconsciously, we all do. Well, they probably would have objected for me to go with Data Boy. They probably would have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they money free? Were they money to keep the non -Catholics, boys that were Catholic?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No. Let that be. Yeah. No. That wasn't a question asked at all. But my father, who was a strong Catholic, had married a non -Catholic. You see. Well, that's right. He mother. So, you see, he really did. You didn't have any blood to stand on. No, he didn't have any blood to stand on. And my mother thought that they should plead to sell those things. But they probably would have. I would say that most, that is probably true in most races. Mm -hmm. No. I'll eat for some. That they do. Because I have known some of them. You see, she doesn't like it. But the depths of this racial prejudice are really... Pretty bad. Yes, it's crazy. It's a very, it's a very tragic thing. That goodness that at least there were certain rights. Because from that, at least they're the way to live. I know that I belong to a crime. There were several, quite a few Southern people in it. I've got a lot of difference in theory about it. And there was this one legal singer, a prominent one, who had been refused service and passed the one. There was a lawsuit that she won, finally. It was quite a thing. And another one in the country, I think they're making too much fuss about that. And she said, no, I don't. The other one who was suddenly, she should have been served. That is ridiculous. You wouldn't like to go with that food. Well, she hadn't thought about it quite that way. And I can remember being kind of social. It was a fundraising thing in World War I, I think. It was in World War I. And there was a Spanish lady came and sang. Well, Spanish people, the best of them probably had some Moroccan blood from the time of that. So they are very dark, some of them. And this woman speaks Castilian, Spanish. She did meet her husband in Mexico. Her family had moved to Mexico. But her Spanish is not Mexican Spanish. It's Castilian, which would tell you something right there. She married a blonde, but her children are all quite dark. And she was dark. And she had sung off. So she sang. But this other one was, my mother said, hill folk. Southern. She didn't take too much ever. And she kept saying, well, of course it's all right. But she shouldn't be sitting in here. And she didn't. Mother said, she'll hear it. I don't care. She shouldn't be doing like that. And my mother, she's dark. Of course, mother didn't know how to handle it. Now, I would tell you a different thing. I would tell you a different thing. Oh, she's a friend of mine. Whether she was a knock, I think. And then they would shut up at least because I learned that you knew it that way. But mother was so embarrassed. She was just embarrassed as fuck for you. And she didn't know what to do, you know, because she kept fussing. Just kept fussing. It's that grocery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have a workshop?

speaker: Catherine Mahon
words: No, I don't. Oh, that's the exact time.
